concept_name: The Direction of the Resultant — tan alpha = B sin theta / (A + B cos theta)
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.2 — direction formula for the resultant; split from vector_addition bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Two small tugboats guiding a large ship into Mumbai Port. One tug pulls with four tonnes almost straight ahead. The second pulls with three tonnes at an angle. The ship's actual path tilts off the first tug's line — the tilt is not midway between the two, it is pulled closer to the stronger tug. The precise tilt angle comes from one formula.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A football player hit by two opposing players at the same instant, one from the front and one from the side. The player falls in a direction that leans toward the stronger hit — the exact lean angle depends on both magnitudes and the angle between the pushes.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A cricket ball in a spinning toss at Eden Gardens. Gravity pulls it straight down, Magnus force from the spin pushes it sideways. The ball travels along the resultant — and its tilt from the vertical is set by the same tan-alpha formula.

alpha_deg.formula: atan2(B * sin(theta_deg * PI / 180), A + B * cos(theta_deg * PI / 180)) * 180 / PI

formulas.direction_formula: tan alpha = B sin theta / (A + B cos theta)
formulas.geometric_meaning: alpha is the angle between R and A, measured at the common tail

constraints: alpha is measured from vector A, rotating toward vector B
Numerator B sin theta = perpendicular leg of the right triangle
Denominator A + B cos theta = horizontal leg (A plus the projection of B on A)
At theta = 0, alpha = 0 (R aligned with A and B). At theta = 180, alpha = 0 or 180 depending on whether A > B or A < B
R does NOT bisect the angle between A and B in general — it bisects only when |A| = |B|

STATE_1.title: Where Does the Ship Actually Go?

scene_composition.text:
Two tugs, two angles of pull.

The ship moves along R
(resultant) in the green direction.

Question: at exactly what
angle alpha does R tilt off
the direction of A?

Half of 60 = 30?
Equal tilt toward each?
No — B is weaker,
so R stays closer to A.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two small tugboats guide a large cargo ship into harbour. Tug one pulls along the ship's axis with four tonnes. Tug two pulls from the side with three tonnes at sixty degrees.
The ship moves along the resultant direction — that is already built into the parallelogram law.
But at what exact angle does it tilt away from tug one? The first instinct is to split the sixty degrees in half and say thirty. That would be wrong.
The resultant does not sit midway. It sits closer to the stronger tug, because the stronger one has more say in where the ship goes.
The precise angle depends on BOTH magnitudes and the angle between them. The next two states derive the exact formula.

STATE_2.title: Drop a Perpendicular — Build the Right Triangle

scene_composition.text:
|  B sin theta
|  = 3 * sin 60
|  = 2.60

A + B cos theta
= 4 + 3 * cos 60
= 4 + 1.5
= 5.50
alpha

Right triangle formed by
- R (hypotenuse)
- A + B cos theta (adjacent to alpha)
- B sin theta (opposite to alpha)

alpha = angle of R from A.

Basic trig ->
tan alpha = opposite / adjacent
          = B sin theta / (A + B cos theta)

tts_sentences.text_en: Use the same parallelogram picture. The key insight is to find a right triangle involving alpha, the angle we want.
From the tip of the shifted B — the far corner of the parallelogram — drop a perpendicular onto the line of A extended.
The perpendicular leg has length B sine theta. For our numbers, three times sine sixty is about two point six.
The horizontal leg starts at the origin and runs to the foot of that perpendicular. Its length is A plus B cosine theta — four plus three times one-half, which is five point five.
The resultant R is the hypotenuse. The angle between R and A is alpha. That is the angle we want.
Basic trigonometry finishes the job. Tangent of alpha equals opposite over adjacent — which is B sine theta divided by A plus B cosine theta. Tap continue.

scene_composition.text: Memorise this form — reference vector matters.

tan alpha  =  B sin theta
              ------------
              A + B cos theta

Reading the formula

  alpha  =  angle of R from A
  A      =  the REFERENCE vector
  B      =  the OTHER vector
  theta  =  angle from A to B

Numerator has B — the 'other'
vector's perpendicular contribution.

Denominator has A plus B cos theta —
A itself, plus B's projection on A.

Watch out

Reversing which vector is A
and which is B changes alpha.

Always note which vector you
measure alpha FROM.

JEE questions usually specify
- 'angle with A'
- 'angle with horizontal'
- 'angle with the direction of motion'

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the formula in its final form. Tangent of alpha equals B sine theta divided by A plus B cosine theta.
Read it carefully. Alpha is measured from A, the reference vector. The numerator carries B — the other vector's perpendicular influence. The denominator carries A itself plus B's projection along A.
Notice the asymmetry. If you swap which vector you call A and which you call B, the formula gives a DIFFERENT alpha. Alpha is always measured from one specific vector.
In JEE problems, the question will specify the reference — 'angle with A', 'angle with horizontal', 'angle with the direction of motion'. Set the matching vector as A in the formula.

STATE_4.title: Solve for the Ship — alpha = ? at theta = 60

scene_composition.text:
tan alpha = B sin theta / (A + B cos theta)
          = 3 * sin 60 / (4 + 3 * cos 60)
          = 2.60 / 5.50
          = 0.472

alpha = tan^(-1) (0.472)
      ~ 25.3 deg

R tilts 25.3 deg off A,
NOT 30 deg.

Tug 1 (stronger) wins more
influence over the ship's path.

tts_sentences.text_en: Apply the formula to the two-tug scene.
Numerator — B times sine theta — is three times sine sixty, about two point six.
Denominator — A plus B cosine theta — is four plus one point five, which is five point five.
Tangent alpha is two point six divided by five point five, about zero point four seven.
Taking the inverse tangent, alpha is about twenty-five degrees.

Not thirty, not forty-five. Twenty-five. The ship's path tilts closer to tug one because tug one is stronger. The weaker tug gets only five degrees of its own sixty-degree angle represented in the final direction.

epic_c_branches.misconception: R bisects the angle between A and B — alpha is half of theta

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — R Sits in the Middle

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

Theta between A and B is 60 deg.
By symmetry, R should sit
exactly halfway between them.

=> alpha = 30 deg.

Appealing — and wrong whenever
A and B have different magnitudes.

tts_sentences.text_en: A very common student intuition — the resultant splits the angle between A and B in half.
For two forces at sixty degrees apart, that would put R at thirty degrees from each.
The intuition is based on symmetry. But the symmetry only exists when the two magnitudes are equal. When A differs from B, the symmetry breaks.

scene_composition.text: 25.3° ≠ 30° — the bisector is wrong here.

Formula
tan alpha = B sin theta / (A + B cos theta)

With A = 4, B = 3, theta = 60 deg

  numerator   = 3 * 0.866 = 2.60
  denominator = 4 + 3 * 0.5 = 5.50
  tan alpha   = 2.60 / 5.50 = 0.472

alpha = 25.3 deg  (NOT 30 deg)

Alpha depends on BOTH
magnitudes, not just theta.

Bisector DOES work when A = B

A = B = 5, theta = 60
  tan alpha = 5 * sin 60 / (5 + 5 * cos 60)
            = 4.33 / 7.5
            = 0.577
  alpha = 30 deg = theta / 2

Equal magnitudes restore
symmetry — that is the only case.

tts_sentences.text_en: Let the formula settle the dispute. Plug in A equals four, B equals three, theta equals sixty.
Tan alpha comes out to about zero point four seven, and alpha is about twenty-five degrees.
Not thirty. The bisector was five degrees off for these numbers, and the gap grows as A and B become more unequal.
Now test the symmetric case — A equals B equals five. Plug in and tan alpha equals zero point five seven, alpha equals thirty, which is exactly theta over two.
So the bisector rule is true only when the magnitudes are equal. Any other time, use the full formula and expect R to lean toward the stronger vector.

STATE_3.title: Mental Model — The Stronger Vector Wins

scene_composition.text: Keep this mental model for every problem.

Intuition you can keep

R ALWAYS leans toward the
stronger of the two vectors.

A > B   =>  alpha < theta / 2
              (R closer to A)

A < B   =>  alpha > theta / 2
              (R closer to B)

A = B   =>  alpha = theta / 2
              (R bisects)

For precise value, use the formula.

Quick sanity check

If your calculated alpha puts R
on the wrong side of theta/2,
you likely swapped A and B.
Recompute.

tts_sentences.text_en: Keep the physical intuition — R always leans toward the stronger vector.
If A is stronger than B, alpha comes out less than half of theta — R sits closer to A.
If B is stronger, alpha exceeds half of theta — R tilts toward B.
Equal magnitudes restore the bisector, and you get alpha equals theta over two.
Use this mental check against your calculated alpha. If the answer is on the wrong side of the bisector, you probably swapped A and B. Recompute with the correct assignment.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student measures alpha from B when the problem asks for alpha from A (or vice versa), getting a different numerical answer.
trigger_phrases: does it matter which vector is reference

EPIC_C_1.title: alpha from A vs alpha from B — Two Different Angles

scene_composition.text: Two valid angles — read the question carefully.

Cross-check: alpha + beta = 25.3 + 34.7 = 60 = theta.
The two angles always sum to theta (because both measure from the same resultant).

tts_sentences.text_en: In the formula, A is the reference vector. Swapping which vector you call A flips the result.
For our ship — alpha from A is twenty five point three degrees. Beta, measured from B, is thirty four point seven degrees.
Two different numbers — both correct, for different references. Read the exam question before plugging in.
Cross-check: alpha plus beta equals theta. Twenty five point three plus thirty four point seven is exactly sixty. Always true — use it to verify your answer.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student applies tan^-1 naively when theta > 90 and the denominator becomes negative — loses the quadrant.

EPIC_C_1.title: When theta > 90, the Denominator Can Go Negative

scene_composition.equation:
A=3, B=5, theta=120 deg
Num = 5*sin(120) = 4.33
Den = 3 + 5*cos(120) = 3 + (-2.5) = 0.5  (>0 still here)
tan(alpha) = 4.33 / 0.5 = 8.66 -> alpha = 83.4 deg
R tilts far past the bisector — because B is bigger.

scene_composition.text:
If denominator goes NEGATIVE (e.g. A=1, B=5, theta=120):
Den = 1 - 2.5 = -1.5.
tan^-1 alone gives alpha in wrong quadrant.
Use atan2(num, den) — it handles sign and gives alpha in correct quadrant (0..180).

tts_sentences.text_en: When theta exceeds ninety degrees, cosine theta becomes negative. The denominator A plus B cosine theta can drop toward zero — and in some cases go negative.
A naive inverse tangent function returns an angle in the wrong quadrant when the denominator flips sign.
Use atan2 of numerator and denominator instead. It tracks the sign of both and returns alpha in the correct zero to one eighty range.
For calculator work: always compute numerator and denominator separately. If denominator is negative, add one hundred eighty degrees to the raw inverse tangent result.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student computes alpha and beta independently and finds alpha + beta ≠ theta — assumes one calculation is wrong.
trigger_phrases: alpha plus beta should equal theta
why alpha and beta don't add up

scene_composition.equation:
alpha = angle(R, A) = 25.3 deg
beta  = angle(R, B) = 34.7 deg
alpha + beta = 60.0 deg = theta  (verified)

Rule: alpha + beta MUST equal theta for a valid answer.

scene_composition.text:
If alpha + beta differs from theta, one of them is wrong.
Most common cause: computing beta from formula with (A, B) swapped
but forgetting to swap theta (theta stays the same — it is between A and B regardless).

tts_sentences.text_en: The resultant R lies between A and B. The angle from A to R is alpha. The angle from R to B is beta. Together they span the full angle theta.
So alpha plus beta must always equal theta. This is a free sanity check for every direction calculation you do.
For the ship: alpha is twenty five point three, beta is thirty four point seven. They add to sixty, exactly equal to theta.
If your computed alpha and beta do NOT add to theta, one of them is wrong. Most often students swap A and B for beta but leave theta alone — theta is the same in both formulas, do not change it.

regeneration_variants.label: Different magnitudes and angles
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 6, B = 8, theta = 45 deg. alpha about 34 deg — R tilts toward stronger B.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Formula tan alpha = B sin theta / (A + B cos theta) applies unchanged

regeneration_variants.label: Perpendicular case — clean numerical sanity check
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 4, B = 3, theta = 90 deg. Numerator 3. Denominator 4. tan alpha = 3/4 = 0.75. alpha = 37 deg. Matches classic 3-4-5 right triangle.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: At right angles, the direction formula reduces to tan alpha = B / A

STATE_1.description: Identify the question: find alpha, the angle R makes with A. State that alpha depends on both magnitudes AND theta.

STATE_2.description: Drop a perpendicular from tip of B (shifted) onto line of A. Identify legs: opposite = B*sin(theta), adjacent = A + B*cos(theta).

STATE_3.description: Apply trig: tan(alpha) = B*sin(theta) / (A + B*cos(theta)). Specify which vector is the reference (A).

STATE_4.description: Numerical example: A=4, B=3, theta=60 -> tan(alpha) = 2.60/5.50 ≈ 0.472 -> alpha ≈ 25.3°. Cross-check alpha + beta = theta.

primitives.text: Step 1: Two vectors A and B at angle theta give resultant R. Find direction of R.
Step 2: alpha = angle between R and reference vector A.
Step 4: Drop perpendicular from tip of shifted B onto line of A.
Step 5: Perpendicular leg = B*sin(theta) (opposite to alpha).
Step 6: Horizontal leg = A + B*cos(theta) (adjacent to alpha).
Step 7: tan(alpha) = opposite / adjacent = B*sin(theta) / (A + B*cos(theta)).
Step 9: A is the reference. Swapping A and B changes alpha.
Step 12: alpha ≈ 25.3°. Cross-check: beta ≈ 34.7°, alpha + beta = 60° = theta.

shortcuts.rule: Direction of resultant from reference A
shortcuts.use_when: Angle of R from A is asked. Keep A as the reference.

shortcuts.rule: Direction of resultant from reference B
shortcuts.use_when: Angle of R from B is asked. Swap A and B in the main formula; theta is unchanged.
Sanity check — if your computed alpha and beta don't add to theta, one is wrong.
theta = 90: cos(theta) = 0, formula collapses to a simple ratio.
Symmetric case — resultant sits exactly on the bisector.

shortcuts.rule: R leans toward the stronger vector
shortcuts.formula: A > B -> alpha < theta/2;  A < B -> alpha > theta/2
shortcuts.use_when: Quick qualitative check before calculating precisely.

edge_cases.title: theta > 90° — denominator sign
edge_cases.trap: Naive tan^-1 gives wrong-quadrant result when A + B*cos(theta) goes negative.
edge_cases.resolution: Use atan2(num, den) on calculators or programming languages. By hand: if denominator is negative, add 180° to the raw arctan to get alpha in [0°, 180°].

edge_cases.title: theta = 180° (antiparallel)
edge_cases.trap: Students assume alpha is 90° or undefined.
edge_cases.resolution: If A > B: R aligns with A, alpha = 0°. If A < B: R aligns with B, alpha = 180°. If A = B: R = 0 and alpha is undefined.

edge_cases.title: theta → 0° (nearly parallel)
edge_cases.trap: Students compute alpha = 0 too early, ignoring small tilt.
edge_cases.resolution: For small theta: tan(alpha) ≈ B*theta / (A + B), so alpha ≈ B*theta/(A+B). R is nearly along A but tilts slightly toward B.

edge_cases.title: Problem says 'angle with horizontal' but A is not horizontal
edge_cases.trap: Students apply the formula without first aligning A with the asked reference.
edge_cases.resolution: Resolve A into components along the asked reference direction. Then compute alpha relative to that reference — not to A.

edge_cases.title: One vector magnitude = 0
edge_cases.trap: Students try to apply the formula — denominator becomes A or B only.
edge_cases.resolution: If B = 0, R = A, alpha = 0 trivially. If A = 0, R = B, alpha = theta. No formula needed — handle as a degenerate case.

formulas_shortform.lean_rule: R leans toward the stronger vector
